verses.commentary: By the Lake of Gennesaret (Sea of Galilee). Crowd pressing to hear the word of God. The crowd's hunger drives the next scene.
Two boats by the lake; fishermen washing nets — failed night shift. The setting is exhausted, unproductive labor.
Gets into Simon's boat — asks him to put out a little. Sits and teaches from the boat. Floating pulpit.
'Put out into the deep, let down nets for a catch.' From teaching to commanding. Fishing instruction from a carpenter to a fisherman.
Simon: 'Master (epistata, ἐπιστάτα), we toiled all night and took nothing. But at your word, I will let down the nets.' Resignation transformed into obedience.
Caught a great number of fish — nets began to break. The empty-night reverses in moments.
Signal partners in other boat. Filled both boats so they began to sink. Superabundance pressing the limits of containment.
Simon Peter falls at Jesus' knees: 'Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord.' Holiness exposure prompts request for distance. Worship and self-knowledge merge.
Astonishment (thambos, θάμβος, awe) at the catch. The miracle's scale exceeds their categories.
James and John, Simon's partners. Jesus: 'Do not fear; from now on you will be catching men (zogron, ζωγρῶν, taking alive).' Their old skill becomes the analogue for new mission.
Brought boats to land, left everything (aphentes panta, ἀφέντες πάντα), followed him. The miraculous catch is given up to follow the catch-giver.
In one of the cities — man full of leprosy. Falls on face, begs: 'Lord, if you will, you can make me clean.' Same as Matthew 8:2.
Stretches out hand, touches him (forbidden under Torah unless one were ceremonially prepared). 'I will; be clean.' Leprosy leaves immediately.
Charged to tell no one, but show himself to the priest, offer the sacrifice Moses commanded (Leviticus 14), for a testimony. Legal validation requested.
News spreads further. Crowds gather to hear and be healed. Restraint backfires; the messianic concealment ebbs and flows.
Jesus often withdrew to desolate places to pray. Lukan distinctive — the prayer-life of Jesus is repeatedly observed.

Pharisees and teachers of the Law sitting (from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem — they came from everywhere). 'The power of the Lord was with him to heal.' Surveillance audience meets healing power.
Men bring a paralytic on a stretcher, try to bring him in. The faith of the bearers is the engine.
Crowd blocks the door. Up to the roof, lower him through the tiles into the middle, before Jesus. Persistent creativity.
Seeing their faith: 'Man, your sins are forgiven you.' Forgiveness announced before healing. The deeper need addressed first.
Pharisees and scribes mentally object: 'Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God alone?' Their question is correct — only God forgives — but their conclusion is wrong.
Jesus, knowing their thoughts: 'Why do you question in your hearts?' Mind-reading is divine prerogative.
Which is easier — to say 'sins forgiven' or 'rise and walk'? The empirical question.
'That you may know the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins' — to the paralytic: 'Rise, take your bed, go home.' Visible miracle authenticates invisible claim.
Immediately rose, took the bed, went home glorifying God. Praise as exit-doxology.
Amazement seized all, glorifying God, full of fear (phobos, φόβος): 'We have seen extraordinary things (paradoxa, παράδοξα) today.' The day-marker semeron implied — eschatological today.
Goes out, sees Levi the tax collector at the tax booth: 'Follow me.' Direct command. Levi (later Matthew) heard.
Leaving everything (katalipon panta, καταλιπὼν πάντα), he rose, followed. Echo of 5:11 — the leave-all pattern.
Great feast in Levi's house — large company of tax collectors and others reclining with them. Levi's evangelism uses his network.
Pharisees and scribes grumble (egogguzon, ἐγόγγυζον — wilderness verb again) to the disciples: 'Why do you eat with tax collectors and sinners?' Table fellowship as theological issue.
Jesus: 'Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick.' Reframing of his presence.
'I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.' Repentance (metanoia, μετάνοια) — Lukan addition emphasizes the transformative reception.
Question about fasting: John's disciples and Pharisees fast — yours eat and drink. Asceticism comparison.
'Can you make the wedding guests fast while the bridegroom is with them?' Bridegroom imagery. Festive presence does not occasion fasting.
'The days will come when the bridegroom is taken away; then in those days they will fast.' First hint of removal. Future fasting is appropriate.
Parable: no one tears piece from a new garment to patch an old. The new tears the old; the patch from the new will not match.
No one puts new wine into old wineskins; new wine bursts the skins. Both lost.
New wine must be put into fresh wineskins. New requires new containers. Kingdom theology requires new structures.

'No one after drinking old wine desires new — for he says, The old is good.' Conservatism explained: the palate prefers what it knows. Resistance to Jesus is structural — the established taste prefers the old vintage.
